items.title: Attention Is All You Need

items.span_text:
Algorithm 1: Adam, our proposed algorithm for stochastic optimization. See section 2 for details,
and for a slightly more efficient (but less clear) order of computation. g _ { t } ^ { 2 } indicates the elementwise
square g _ { t } \odot g _ { t } . Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are \alpha = 0 . 0 0 1
\bar { \beta _ { 1 } } = 0 \bar { . } 9 , \beta _ { 2 } \bar { = } 0 . 999 and \epsilon = 1 0 ^ { - 8 } . All operations on vectors are element-wise. With \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } and \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t }
we denote \beta _ { 1 } and \beta _ { 2 } to the power t .
Require: \alpha \mathrm { : } Stepsize
Require: \beta _ { 1 } , \bar { \beta _ { 2 } } \overset { - } { \in } [ 0 , 1 ) : Exponential decay rates for the moment estimates
Require: f ( \theta ) { \mathrm { : } } Stochastic objective function with parameters θ
Require: \theta _ { 0 } \colon Initial parameter vector
m _ { 0 } \gets 0 (Initialize 1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } } moment vector)
v _ { 0 } 0 (Initialize 2 ^ { \mathrm { n d } } moment vector)
t \gets 0 (Initialize timestep)
while \theta _ { t } not converged do
t \gets t + 1
g _ { t } \gets \nabla _ { \theta } f _ { t } ( \theta _ { t - 1 } ) (Get gradients w.r.t. stochastic objective at timestep t)
m _ { t } \gets \beta _ { 1 } \cdot m _ { t - 1 } \dot { + } \left( 1 \bar { - } \beta _ { 1 } \right) \cdot g _ { t } (Update biased first moment estimate)
v _ { t } \beta _ { 2 } \cdot v _ { t - 1 } + ( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ) \cdot g _ { t } ^ { 2 } (Update biased second raw moment estimate)
\widehat { m } _ { t } \gets m _ { t } / ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } ) (Compute bias-corrected first moment estimate)
\widehat { v } _ { t } \gets v _ { t } / ( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t } ) (Compute bias-corrected second raw moment estimate)
\theta _ { t } \theta _ { t - 1 } - \alpha \cdot \widehat { m } _ { t } / ( \sqrt { v _ { t } } + \epsilon ) (Update parameters)
end while
return { \underline { { \theta _ { t } } } } (Resulting parameters)
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

final_answer.markdown:
There is no real conflict — the two values live at different scopes, and the corpus confirms both.

**The Adam default (0.999).** In Algorithm 1 of the Adam paper (Kingma & Ba, 2014, p. 2), the authors write: "Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are α = 0.001, β₁ = 0.9, β₂ = 0.999 and ε = 10⁻⁸." [1] So 0.999 is the algorithm's recommended default across the problems the authors tested.

**The Transformer's choice (0.98).** In §5.3 "Optimizer" of the Transformer paper, Vaswani et al. state that they used the Adam optimizer with β₁ = 0.9, β₂ = 0.98, and ε = 10⁻⁹. [2] So the Transformer does, in fact, override the default and use 0.98.

**Adjudication.** The two claims are consistent once their scopes are distinguished:

- 0.999 is Adam's *general default* — a safe starting point recommended in Algorithm 1 of the Adam paper. [1]
- 0.98 is what the Transformer authors *chose* in §5.3 of Vaswani et al. 2017, deliberately overriding that default for their specific architecture and training setup. [2]

Sources that say "0.999" are paraphrasing the Adam paper's general default. Sources that say "0.98" are quoting the Transformer paper. Neither is wrong — they are answering different questions (default vs. specific choice). The apparent conflict dissolves once the scope of each value is made explicit.

Sources
[1] Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization, unsectioned, p. 2
[2] Attention Is All You Need, 5.3 Optimizer, p. 7

locations.preview: We trained our models on one machine with 8 NVIDIA P100 GPUs. For our base models using the hyperparameters described throughout the paper, each training step took about 0.4 seconds. We trained the base models for a total of 100,000 steps or 12 hours. For our big models,(described on the bottom line of table 3), step time was 1.0 seconds. The big models were trained for 300,000 steps (3.5 days).
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: For the base models, we used a single model obtained by averaging the last 5 checkpoints, which were written at 10-minute intervals. For the big models, we averaged the last 20 checkpoints. We used beam search with a beam size of 4 and length penalty $\alpha = 0 . 6 [ 3 8 ]$ . These hyperparameters were chosen after experimentation on the development set. We set the maximum output length during inference to input length + 50, but terminate early when possible [38].
locations.title: Attention Is All You Need

locations.preview: This corresponds to increasing the learning rate linearly for the first warmup\_steps training steps, and decreasing it thereafter proportionally to the inverse square root of the step number. We used warmup $. s t e p s = 4 0 0 0$

arguments.markdown:
## Resolved: 0.999 is Adam's default; 0.98 is the Transformer's task-specific choice

There is no real conflict — the two values live at different scopes.

**What the Transformer paper actually says** (Section 5.3 "Optimizer", p. 7):

> "We used the Adam optimizer [20] with β₁ = 0.9, β₂ = 0.98 and ε = 10⁻⁹." [[ev_c478868f3b1b547e]]

So Vaswani et al. (2017) do, in fact, use **β₂ = 0.98** for their Transformer experiments.

**Where 0.999 comes from**

β₂ = 0.999 is the value recommended in the *original Adam paper* by Kingma & Ba (2014), which the Transformer cites as reference [20]. It is the algorithm's general default, intended as a safe starting point across a wide range of problems.

**Adjudication**

| Claim | Source | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| β₂ = 0.999 | Kingma & Ba 2014 (Adam paper, ref [20]) | Default / general-purpose setting |
| β₂ = 0.98 | Vaswani et al. 2017, §5.3 | Specific choice for the Transformer architecture |

The Transformer authors deliberately overrode the Adam default. The two statements are consistent once their scopes are distinguished: 0.999 is what Adam *suggests by default*; 0.98 is what the Transformer authors *chose to use* for their setup. Sources that say "0.999" are paraphrasing Adam's general default; sources that say "0.98" are quoting the Transformer paper. Neither is wrong — they are answering different questions.

arguments.markdown:
There is no real conflict — the two values live at different scopes, and the corpus confirms which one the Transformer actually used.

In Section 5.3 "Optimizer" of the Transformer paper, Vaswani et al. state that they used the Adam optimizer with β₁ = 0.9, β₂ = 0.98, and ε = 10⁻⁹. [[ev_c478868f3b1b547e]] So the Transformer does, in fact, use β₂ = 0.98 for its experiments.

The 0.999 figure, by contrast, is the *general default* for Adam's second-moment decay rate as documented in Adam's original publication. That paper is not in the corpus, so this resolution can be stated only on the basis of general knowledge about Adam's defaults; the corpus-side fact is the 0.98 quoted above.

Adjudication:

- 0.999 is Adam's general default (the algorithm's recommended starting value, documented externally to the corpus).
- 0.98 is what the Transformer authors *chose* in §5.3 of Vaswani et al. 2017, overriding that default for their architecture and training setup.

Sources that say 0.999 are paraphrasing Adam's general default; sources that say 0.98 are quoting the Transformer paper. Neither is wrong — they are answering different questions. The corpus definitively supports the 0.98 value for the Transformer.
